nativeRange.habitat: Seasonal wetlands, rice paddies, and shallow river margins across tropical West Africa

decorPreferences: midground to background placement with vertical room to grow
high lighting (PAR ~30-50) to develop copper-red coloration

summary: A demanding red-to-copper background stem plant from tropical West Africa; generally considered a showpiece red Ammannia for high-tech planted tanks where strong light, CO2, and iron-rich fertilization bring out pink-orange to brick-red foliage.
careNotes: Ammannia senegalensis is a stem plant native to tropical West Africa (especially Senegal and Mali, where it often grows as a rice-paddy weed) and is sold in the hobby under trade names like 'Red Ammannia' and 'Copper Leaf Ammannia'; the older spelling 'Ammania senegalensis' is still common in retail and care guides. It is sometimes confused with its larger relative Ammannia gracilis (fw-plant-053) but is a distinct species — generally described as more compact in leaf size and habit, though under good conditions it still grows tall enough to function as a background plant. Sources broadly agree on a temperature range around 22-28 °C and soft-to-moderately-hard water (gH ~4-12 dGH), with pH most commonly cited as 6.5-7.5 (some sources prefer 6.5-7.0). Adult height is the main point of cross-source disagreement: Buce Plant lists 3-5 in (~8-13 cm) — likely shipped size — while Aqua-Fish.net and other hobby guides describe 30-45 cm at full submerged height; the file uses a 15-45 cm range to span both shipped and mature in-tank dimensions. To develop the characteristic pink-orange to copper-red coloration the plant is sold for, it generally needs high light (~30-50 PAR, 8-10 h photoperiod), pressurized CO2 (~25-30 mg/L), and consistent iron + potassium dosing alongside macro fertilization; under low light it survives but stays green-yellow with weak color. Best red colour is reported when nitrates run lean while phosphate and micros remain plentiful. The plant prefers a nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil or root tabs) and is sensitive to abrupt parameter swings — sudden changes can trigger leaf melt, especially on freshly planted tissue-culture stems transitioning from emersed to submersed growth. Note: Buce Plant lists the family as Onagraceae, but Wikipedia, Aqua-Fish.net, and standard botanical references confirm Lythraceae (order Myrtales) — the schema records the correct Lythraceae.
breedingNotes: Propagated by stem cuttings: cut the newest growth (about 5-10 cm with a few leaves) from a mature stem and replant in the substrate. Trimming the upper half of an established stem encourages bushier lateral side-shoot growth on the mother plant. When grown emersed, the plant produces small inconspicuous flowers from the leaf axils, but seed propagation is uncommon in the hobby.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Used for West African origin, care level (difficult), light (moderate to high), CO2 (recommended), growth rate (medium), placement (foreground/midground for shipped size), pH (6.5-7.5), propagation by cuttings, and coloration notes (brick red predominant, with green/orange/yellow accents). Buce Plant's listed family 'Onagraceae' is incorrect — corrected to Lythraceae based on Wikipedia and Aqua-Fish.net.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (71-83 °F / 21.7-28.3 °C), care level (moderate, intermediate-to-advanced), lighting (moderate to strong), CO2 (recommended), growth rate (fast under good conditions), background placement, and coloration (light green with copper tones through deep red/maroon/crimson).

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Lythraceae), native range (Senegal, Mali), adult size (30-45 cm), temperature (20-26 °C), pH (6.5-7.0), hardness (4-12 dGH), care level (medium-difficult), high lighting, CO2 strongly recommended, nutrient-rich substrate, iron and potassium dosing, and 10 cm stem-cutting propagation.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used to confirm taxonomy (Lythraceae, Myrtales) and native range (western Senegal, rice-paddy weed habitat), correcting Buce Plant's incorrect Onagraceae assignment.
